The **University of Chicago** (**UChicago**, **Chicago**, **UChi**, or **U of C**) is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [research university](/wiki/Research_university "Research university") in the [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Chicago "Hyde Park, Chicago") community area of [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), Illinois, United States.

The university is composed of an [undergraduate college](/wiki/College_of_the_University_of_Chicago "College of the University of Chicago") and [four graduate research divisions](/wiki/Divisions_of_the_University_of_Chicago "Divisions of the University of Chicago"): the Arts & Humanities Division, the Biological Sciences Division, the Physical Sciences Division, and the Social Sciences Division, all of which include various organized departments and institutes. In addition, the university operates seven professional schools in the fields of [business](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Booth_School_of_Business "University of Chicago Booth School of Business"), [social work](/wiki/Crown_Family_School_of_Social_Work,_Policy,_and_Practice "Crown Family School of Social Work, Policy, and Practice"), [theology](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Divinity_School "University of Chicago Divinity School"), [public policy](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Harris_School_of_Public_Policy "University of Chicago Harris School of Public Policy"), [law](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Law_School "University of Chicago Law School"), [medicine](/wiki/Pritzker_School_of_Medicine "Pritzker School of Medicine"), and [molecular engineering](/wiki/Pritzker_School_of_Molecular_Engineering "Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering"), as well as a school of [continuing studies](/wiki/Graham_School_of_Continuing_Liberal_and_Professional_Studies "Graham School of Continuing Liberal and Professional Studies"). The university maintains satellite campuses and centers in London, [Hong Kong](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Hong_Kong "University of Chicago Hong Kong"), Paris, Beijing, Delhi, Luxor, and downtown Chicago.

University of Chicago scholars have played a role in the development of many academic disciplines, including economics, law, literary criticism, mathematics, physics, religion, sociology, and political science, establishing the [Chicago schools](/wiki/Chicago_school_(disambiguation) "Chicago school (disambiguation)") of thought in various fields. The university's [Metallurgical Laboratory](/wiki/Metallurgical_Laboratory "Metallurgical Laboratory") produced the world's first human-made, self-sustaining nuclear reaction in [Chicago Pile-1](/wiki/Chicago_Pile-1 "Chicago Pile-1") beneath the viewing stands of the university's [Stagg Field](/wiki/Stagg_Field "Stagg Field"). Advances in chemistry led to the "radiocarbon revolution" in the [carbon-14 dating](/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating "Radiocarbon dating") of ancient life and objects. The university operates and administers research institutions including the [Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory](/wiki/Fermilab "Fermilab"), the [Argonne National Laboratory](/wiki/Argonne_National_Laboratory "Argonne National Laboratory"), and the [Marine Biological Laboratory](/wiki/Marine_Biological_Laboratory "Marine Biological Laboratory"). The university is also home to the [University of Chicago Press](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Press "University of Chicago Press"), the largest [university press](/wiki/List_of_university_presses "List of university presses") in North America.

As of 2025, the university's students, faculty, and staff have included [101 Nobel laureates](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_by_university_affiliation "List of Nobel laureates by university affiliation"). The university's [faculty members and alumni](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Chicago_people "List of University of Chicago people") also include 10 [Fields Medalists](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal"), 4 [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award") winners, 58 [MacArthur Fellows](/wiki/MacArthur_Fellows_Program "MacArthur Fellows Program"), 30 [Marshall Scholars](/wiki/Marshall_Scholars "Marshall Scholars"), 55 [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholarship "Rhodes Scholarship"), 27 [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") winners, 20 [National Humanities Medalists](/wiki/National_Humanities_Medal "National Humanities Medal"), and 6 [Olympic medalists](/wiki/List_of_American_universities_with_Olympic_medals "List of American universities with Olympic medals").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of the University of Chicago](/wiki/History_of_the_University_of_Chicago "History of the University of Chicago")

### Old University of Chicago

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Old University of Chicago")]

Further information: [Old University of Chicago](/wiki/Old_University_of_Chicago "Old University of Chicago")

[![A professor addressing a crowd](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Uchicago_convocation_1894.jpg/250px-Uchicago_convocation_1894.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uchicago_convocation_1894.jpg)

[Albert A. Michelson](/wiki/Albert_A._Michelson "Albert A. Michelson"), professor of physics and the first American Nobel laureate, delivers the second [convocation](/wiki/Convocation "Convocation") address in front of Goodspeed and Gates-Blake Halls, with President [William Rainey Harper](/wiki/William_Rainey_Harper "William Rainey Harper"), professors, and trustees in attendance, July 1, 1894.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The first University of Chicago was founded by a small group of [Baptist](/wiki/Baptists_in_the_United_States "Baptists in the United States") educators and incorporated in 1857 after a land endowment from Senator [Stephen A. Douglas](/wiki/Stephen_A._Douglas "Stephen A. Douglas") and a fundraising campaign directed by the first president of the institution, [John C. Burroughs](/wiki/John_C._Burroughs "John C. Burroughs").[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 8–10 It closed in 1886 after decades of financial struggle, exacerbated by the [Great Chicago Fire](/wiki/Great_Chicago_Fire "Great Chicago Fire") and the [Panic of 1873](/wiki/Panic_of_1873 "Panic of 1873"),[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 18 when the university's property was foreclosed on by its creditors.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 8–10 In 1890, its trustees elected to change the university's name to the "[Old University of Chicago](/wiki/Old_University_of_Chicago "Old University of Chicago")" so that the new university could go by the name of the city; a year later, the new university voted to recognize the alumni of the old as alumni of the new.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 59

### Early years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early years")]

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/Wikisource-logo.svg/40px-Wikisource-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wikisource-logo.svg)

[Wikisource](/wiki/Wikisource "Wikisource") has the text of a [1911 *Encyclopædia Britannica*](/wiki/Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica_Eleventh_Edition "Encyclopædia Britannica Eleventh Edition") article about [the founding and early years](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/1911_Encyclop%C3%A6dia_Britannica/Chicago,_University_of "wikisource:1911 Encyclopædia Britannica/Chicago, University of").

In 1890, the [American Baptist Education Society](/wiki/American_Baptist_Churches_USA "American Baptist Churches USA") (ABES) incorporated a new University of Chicago as a [coeducational](/wiki/Mixed-sex_education "Mixed-sex education") institution,[[5]](#cite_note-goodspeed2-5): 137 using $400,000 donated to the ABES to supplement a $600,000 donation from [Standard Oil](/wiki/Standard_Oil "Standard Oil") co-founder [John D. Rockefeller](/wiki/John_D._Rockefeller "John D. Rockefeller")[[6]](#cite_note-6) and land donated by [Marshall Field](/wiki/Marshall_Field "Marshall Field").[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 60–61 The [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Chicago "Hyde Park, Chicago") campus’ construction was financed by donations from wealthy Chicagoans such as [Silas B. Cobb](/wiki/Silas_B._Cobb "Silas B. Cobb"), donor of the campus's first building, Cobb Lecture Hall;[[7]](#cite_note-7) [Charles L. Hutchinson](/wiki/Charles_L._Hutchinson "Charles L. Hutchinson"), trustee, treasurer and donor of [Hutchinson Commons](/wiki/Hutchinson_Commons "Hutchinson Commons");[[8]](#cite_note-8) and [Martin A. Ryerson](/wiki/Martin_A._Ryerson "Martin A. Ryerson"), president of the board of trustees and donor of the Ryerson Physical Laboratory.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 103–105

[William Rainey Harper](/wiki/William_Rainey_Harper "William Rainey Harper") became the university's president on July 1, 1891, and classes first began on October 1, 1892.[[9]](#cite_note-frederick2-9) Harper offered large salaries to attract senior faculty,[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 78 and in two years had a faculty of 120, including eight former university or college presidents.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The undergraduate program was divided into two parts, with the first two years making up the Academic College, focusing on preparation for higher learning, and the last two years comprising the University College, with more advanced courses. The university operated on a [quarter system](/wiki/Academic_quarter_(year_division) "Academic quarter (year division)"), with 36 courses required to graduate.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 82–84 Harper brought the [Baptist](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") seminary, which had historical ties to the Old University of Chicago, to the university. This became the [Divinity School](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Divinity_School "University of Chicago Divinity School") in 1891, the first graduate professional school at the University of Chicago.[[11]](#cite_note-goodspeed-11): 20–22 Harper was a supporter of intercollegiate athletics, recruiting [Amos Alonzo Stagg](/wiki/Amos_Alonzo_Stagg "Amos Alonzo Stagg") in 1892 to coach the football team and defending athletics from faculty opposition.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 89 In 1894, the university adopted [maroon](/wiki/Maroon "Maroon") as its official color after initially selecting [goldenrod](/wiki/Goldenrod_(color) "Goldenrod (color)"). The Maroons became the university's nickname during the same year.[[12]](#cite_note-:72-12) During this period, the university founded the [university extension](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Continuing_Education_Division "University of Chicago Continuing Education Division"), which offered evening courses for adults and [correspondence courses](/wiki/Distance_education "Distance education"), and the [University of Chicago Press](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Press "University of Chicago Press").[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 74

Rockefeller continued to provide significant contributions to the university after its founding. Harper's efforts to finance faculty research projects, expand the campus, and support university initiatives caused significant deficits covered by Rockefeller donations, with annual deficits between 1894 and 1903 averaging $215,000. In 1898, the board of trustees made a commitment to use new gifts to eliminate the deficit rather than to further expand programs, but structural deficits remained until after Harper's presidency.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 110–112

### 1906–1929

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1906–1929")]

After Harper's death in 1906, the board of trustees named [Harry Pratt Judson](/wiki/Harry_Pratt_Judson "Harry Pratt Judson"), head of the Department of Political Science, acting president; in 1907, the appointment was made permanent.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 149–150 Judson initiated a policy of financial austerity, which renewed Rockefeller's confidence in the university and resulted in a series of large gifts to the endowment between 1906 and 1910, including a final gift of $10 million in 1910 that balanced the university's budget.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 113 In 1911, the university adopted a Latin motto of *Crescat scientia; vita excolatur*, which translates to "Let knowledge grow from more and more; and so be human life enriched."[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 1912, Judson successfully encouraged the board to create a faculty pension fund.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 150

During [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Judson, as well as faculty members such as [Albion Small](/wiki/Albion_Woodbury_Small "Albion Woodbury Small") and [Paul Shorey](/wiki/Paul_Shorey "Paul Shorey"), published works supporting the war. On the other hand, student reaction was mixed, with most not participating in newly formed voluntary military training programs such as the [ROTC](/wiki/Reserve_Officers%27_Training_Corps "Reserve Officers' Training Corps"). In 1918, the Student Army Training Corps program was announced by the War Department, which requisitioned the campus to be run by army officers for military training, but the [November armistice](/wiki/Armistice_of_11_November_1918 "Armistice of 11 November 1918") soon ended the program.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 154–162 After the war, the Oriental Institute, now the [Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures](/wiki/Institute_for_the_Study_of_Ancient_Cultures "Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures"), was founded by Egyptologist [James Henry Breasted](/wiki/James_Henry_Breasted "James Henry Breasted") to support and interpret [archeological](/wiki/Archeological "Archeological") work in what was then called the [Near East](/wiki/Near_East "Near East").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 1923, senior scholar [Ernest D. Burton](/wiki/Ernest_DeWitt_Burton "Ernest DeWitt Burton") succeeded Judson as president.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 163–164 Burton launched the first major fundraising campaign of the university to improve the research environment of the faculty as well as invest in residential halls for undergraduates, finding initial success despite faculty opposition to the perceived prioritization of undergraduate over graduate interests. Burton's sudden death in 1925 led to his replacement by physicist [Max Mason](/wiki/Max_Mason "Max Mason"), who ended the citywide fundraising drive early in favor of a quieter outreach among local businessmen.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 167–182 During Burton's term, and later Mason's, the [Chicago Schools](/wiki/Chicago_school_(disambiguation) "Chicago school (disambiguation)") of thought began to emerge in the social sciences, with new organizations being established such as the [Social Science Research Council](/wiki/Social_Science_Research_Council "Social Science Research Council") in 1923.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 190–191

### 1929–1950

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 1929–1950")]

[![A group of people in suits standing in three rows on the steps in front of a stone building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/ChicagoPileTeam.png/250px-ChicagoPileTeam.png)](/wiki/File:ChicagoPileTeam.png)

Some of the University of Chicago team who worked on the production of the world's first human-caused self-sustaining nuclear reaction, including [Enrico Fermi](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi "Enrico Fermi") in the front row and [Leó Szilárd](/wiki/Le%C3%B3_Szil%C3%A1rd "Leó Szilárd") in the second

In 1929, the 30-year-old dean of [Yale Law School](/wiki/Yale_Law_School "Yale Law School"), [Robert Maynard Hutchins](/wiki/Robert_Maynard_Hutchins "Robert Maynard Hutchins"), became president.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 167–182 In 1930, Hutchins organized the graduate departments under four independent divisions and united the undergraduate colleges into one college.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 222 In 1931, alongside dean of the college [Chauncey Boucher](/wiki/Chauncey_Samuel_Boucher "Chauncey Samuel Boucher"), Hutchins implemented a new two-year general education curriculum called the "New Plan", which formed the basis for the university's core curriculum.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 231–233 Later in the 1930s, Hutchins became unsatisfied with departmental influence on the undergraduate curriculum and pushed for further expansion to the general education curriculum.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 242–252 In 1942, Hutchins transferred jurisdiction of the BA degree from the graduate divisions to the college, thus removing divisional leverage to shape the curriculum. The same year, the college reformed the BA degree with four years of prescribed general education.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 253–255

Budget shortfalls caused by the Great Depression led to significant austerity measures and staffing cuts, though Hutchins protected the salaries of those who remained. In 1933, Hutchins proposed a plan to alleviate the financial situation by merging the university with [Northwestern University](/wiki/Northwestern_University "Northwestern University"), though it was ultimately abandoned.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 287–289 Financial woes contributed to the decision to end the university's football program in 1939.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 214 With substantial budget gaps remaining and support from the [Rockefeller Foundation](/wiki/Rockefeller_Foundation "Rockefeller Foundation") having dried up, a second major fundraising campaign was launched between 1939 and 1941 to celebrate the fiftieth anniversary of the university's founding with mixed results.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 290–299 Large deficits persisted after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), leaving future presidents to balance the budget.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 316

During the war, the university recruited a number of refugee scientists from Europe, including [Enrico Fermi](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi "Enrico Fermi"), [Rudolf Carnap](/wiki/Rudolf_Carnap "Rudolf Carnap"), and [James Franck](/wiki/James_Franck "James Franck"). The university's [Metallurgical Laboratory](/wiki/Metallurgical_Laboratory "Metallurgical Laboratory") contributed to the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project"), with Enrico Fermi engineering the first [self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction](/wiki/Nuclear_chain_reaction "Nuclear chain reaction") under the stands of [Stagg Field](/wiki/Stagg_Field "Stagg Field"), the university's football stadium, in 1942.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 301–305 In 1945, Hutchins announced the formation of the Institute for Nuclear Studies and the Institute for the Study of Metals in order to continue work done during the war. These were later renamed the [Enrico Fermi Institute](/wiki/Enrico_Fermi_Institute "Enrico Fermi Institute") and the [James Franck Institute](/wiki/James_Franck_Institute "James Franck Institute"), respectively.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 286 The university came under public scrutiny before and after the war for alleged communist influence, with university leadership called to testify before the [Illinois General Assembly](/wiki/Illinois_General_Assembly "Illinois General Assembly") on the loyalty of its student body and faculty in 1935 and 1949.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 269–280

### 1951–1977

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1951–1977")]

In 1951, vice president of development [Lawrence Kimpton](/wiki/Lawrence_A._Kimpton "Lawrence A. Kimpton") succeeded Hutchins as chancellor,[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 323–324 a position created in 1945 replacing the president as head of the university.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 310 The deficits left from Hutchins necessitated severe annual cuts in the operating budget, which was brought into balance by 1954. A fundraising campaign was launched the same year, which allowed for modest recovery,[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 338–343 but the financial situation worsened after a decline in undergraduate enrollments.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 325–327 In 1957, to attract more students, Kimpton reduced the general education curriculum from four years to two years. Furthermore, the graduate divisional faculty with slowly merged with the previously independent college faculty via joint appointments.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 328–330 To address safety concerns driven by increasing crime and poverty in the Hyde Park neighborhood, the university became a major sponsor of a controversial [urban renewal project for Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Chicago#Racial_integration,_economic_decline,_and_urban_renewal "Hyde Park, Chicago"). Between 1954 and 1960, the university worked with the South East Chicago Commission and Mayor [Richard J. Daley](/wiki/Richard_J._Daley "Richard J. Daley") to clear approximately 925 acres of land, disproportionately affecting Black, low-income residents.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 343–350

[![A newspaper whose headline is "UC admits housing segregation"](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Chicago_Maroon_%28January_17%2C_1962%29.pdf/page1-250px-Chicago_Maroon_%28January_17%2C_1962%29.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chicago_Maroon_(January_17,_1962).pdf)

Front page of *[Chicago Maroon](/wiki/The_Chicago_Maroon "The Chicago Maroon")* breaking the news of the university's segregationist off-campus rental policies

In 1961, [Caltech](/wiki/California_Institute_of_Technology "California Institute of Technology") professor [George Beadle](/wiki/George_Beadle "George Beadle") was elected chancellor, resuming the title of president later that year.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 355–356 Beadle's tenure saw large investments in faculty and campus expansion to rebuild the university after Kimpton's austerity, funded in large part by a $25 million grant provided by the [Ford Foundation](/wiki/Ford_Foundation "Ford Foundation") and an accompanying fundraising campaign.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 359–365 The university experienced its share of student unrest during this time, beginning in 1962 when then-freshman [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") helped lead a [15-day sit-in at the college's administration building](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_sit-ins "University of Chicago sit-ins") in a protest over the university's segregationist off-campus rental policies. After continued turmoil, a university committee in 1967 issued what became known as the [Kalven Report](/wiki/Kalven_Report "Kalven Report"). The report, a two-page statement of the university's policy in "social and political action," declared that "To perform its mission in the society, a university must sustain an extraordinary environment of freedom of inquiry and maintain an independence from political fashions, passions, and pressures."[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 1964, the undergraduate college was reorganized into five collegiate divisions, four paralleling the four graduate divisions and one interdisciplinary New Collegiate Division.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 366

In 1967, provost [Edward Levi](/wiki/Edward_H._Levi "Edward H. Levi") became president. His tenure saw a number of sit-ins at the administrative building: in 1962, over the university's segregationist off-campus rental policies;[[20]](#cite_note-20) in 1966 and 1967, over the university providing the class rank of students who sought deferments to draft boards; and in 1969, over the sociology's department decision not to rehire the openly Marxist assistant professor Marlene Dixon.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 371–375 In 1967, a university committee issued the [Kalven Report](/wiki/Kalven_Report "Kalven Report"), maintaining the university's duty to uphold academic freedom and remain non-partisan.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 497–499 The report has since been cited in university debates over divesting from South Africa and Sudan,[[22]](#cite_note-22) as well as in the [Chicago Principles](/wiki/Chicago_Principles "Chicago Principles") on free speech,[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 500 which a number of other universities have since adopted.[[23]](#cite_note-23) By the 1970s, facing the end of the Ford Foundation's support, a reduction in enrollment due to insufficient student housing, flagging federal funding, and broader economic [stagflation](/wiki/1973%E2%80%931975_recession "1973–1975 recession"), the university faced more fiscal austerity.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 379–387 In 1975, provost John Wilson was appointed president, balancing the budget once more through cuts.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 391–393

### 1978–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1978–present")]

In 1978, history scholar and provost of [Yale](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), [Hanna Holborn Gray](/wiki/Hanna_Holborn_Gray "Hanna Holborn Gray"), became president of the university. She was the first woman in the United States to be appointed to a full-term presidency of a major research university.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 393 Still facing budgetary issues, Gray modernized the university's financial systems, increased the size and tuition of the undergraduate college, and paired campus expansion and renovation with administrative austerity. While budgetary equilibrium was reached through the mid-1980s, acute deficits soon re-emerged, exacerbated by the [1990-1992 recession](/wiki/Early_1990s_recession "Early 1990s recession").[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 393–398 Gray also oversaw the implementation of a unified 21 course core curriculum across all collegiate divisions in 1985 and invested in student life through new food services, school festivals, and the reintroduction of varsity athletics.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 403–405

[![A view of neo-Gothic buildings in the background, with a grassy area in front](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/Harper_Midway_Chicago.jpg/250px-Harper_Midway_Chicago.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harper_Midway_Chicago.jpg)

View from the [Midway Plaisance](/wiki/Midway_Plaisance "Midway Plaisance")

In 1992, economist [Hugo F. Sonnenschein](/wiki/Hugo_F._Sonnenschein "Hugo F. Sonnenschein") became president, facing projected deficits of $23 million for the 1995-96 budget and poor endowment growth.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 407 The raising of $676 million in a fundraising campaign for the university's centennial throughout the early 1990s helped alleviate these problems.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 418 In 1996, Sonnenschein proposed the expansion of the undergraduate college by 1,000 students to raise tuition revenue, and in 1997, backed a plan to reduce the number of required course in the core curriculum from 21 to 15–18 (depending on how a student met the language requirement). After intense debate, with the university becoming the focal point of a national debate on education, both reforms were approved.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 409–417 In 2000, [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University") provost Don Michael Randel became the twelfth president of the university. His tenure was marked by increased support for the arts on campus, stronger outreach to local civic and business leaders, investments in major campus facilities, and the launch of a new $2 billion capital campaign.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 421

In 2006, mathematician [Robert J. Zimmer](/wiki/Robert_Zimmer "Robert Zimmer") was appointed president, receiving board approval to take on large amounts of debt at low interest rates after the [2008 recession](/wiki/Great_Recession_in_the_United_States "Great Recession in the United States") in order to finance a number of major projects.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 465–468 These included new buildings and pavilions, such as [Mansueto Library](/wiki/Joe_and_Rika_Mansueto_Library "Joe and Rika Mansueto Library") in 2011, a reading room and book storage facility;[[24]](#cite_note-24) the Logan Center for the Arts in 2012;[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 471 the Keller Center in 2019, home of the [Harris School of Public Policy](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Harris_School_of_Public_Policy "University of Chicago Harris School of Public Policy");[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 471 and Woodlawn Residential Commons in 2020, which houses 1,298 students.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Between 2008 and 2022, the university partnered with the city and outside businesses to launch three interventions along 53rd Street in an attempt to improve the neighborhood's economic condition.[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 473–477 As part of an effort to invest in its professional schools, the university formed the [Becker Friedman Institute](/wiki/Becker_Friedman_Institute_for_Research_in_Economics "Becker Friedman Institute for Research in Economics") in 2011,[[26]](#cite_note-26) acquired the [Marine Biological Laboratory](/wiki/Marine_Biological_Laboratory "Marine Biological Laboratory") in 2013,[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 491 repurposed the [Crerar Library](/wiki/John_Crerar_Library "John Crerar Library") as the headquarters of the Department of Computer Science in 2018,[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 472 and established the [Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering](/wiki/Pritzker_School_of_Molecular_Engineering "Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering") in 2019.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The university also expanded its presence abroad, opening [campuses in Hong Kong](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Hong_Kong "University of Chicago Hong Kong") in 2018,[[28]](#cite_note-28) in London in 2022,[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 507 and in Paris in 2024,[[29]](#cite_note-29) alongside centers in Beijing in 2010[[30]](#cite_note-30) and in Delhi in 2014.[[31]](#cite_note-31) Despite a $5.4 billion fundraising campaign started in 2014,[[4]](#cite_note-:02-4): 468 university debt has exceeded initial planning expectations, reaching $6.3 billion in 2025.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In 2021, Zimmer was succeeded by [Paul Alivisatos](/wiki/Paul_Alivisatos "Paul Alivisatos"), then-provost of the [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley"). In 2024, university students set up [an encampment on the university's main quad](/wiki/List_of_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses_in_the_United_States_in_2024#Illinois "List of pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses in the United States in 2024")[[33]](#cite_note-33) as a part of [the nationwide movement](/wiki/2024_pro-Palestinian_protests_on_university_campuses "2024 pro-Palestinian protests on university campuses") in support of Palestine at institutions of higher learning across the country. The encampment was later cleared by University of Chicago Police Department officers.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Campus")]

### Main campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Main campus")]

[![The campus of the University of Chicago](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/University_of_Chicago_campus_panorama_%282011%29.jpg/1280px-University_of_Chicago_campus_panorama_%282011%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Chicago_campus_panorama_(2011).jpg "The campus of the University of Chicago, from the top of Rockefeller Chapel. The Main Quadrangles can be seen on the left (west), the Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures, West Asia & North Africa and the Becker Friedman Institute for Research in Economics can be seen in the center (north) and the Booth School of Business and Laboratory Schools can be seen on the right (east), as the panoramic is bounded on both sides by the Midway Plaisance (south).")

The campus of the University of Chicago, from the top of [Rockefeller Chapel](/wiki/Rockefeller_Chapel "Rockefeller Chapel"). The Main Quadrangles can be seen on the left (west), the [Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures, West Asia & North Africa](/wiki/Institute_for_the_Study_of_Ancient_Cultures,_West_Asia_%26_North_Africa "Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures, West Asia & North Africa") and the [Becker Friedman Institute for Research in Economics](/wiki/Becker_Friedman_Institute_for_Research_in_Economics "Becker Friedman Institute for Research in Economics") can be seen in the center (north) and the [Booth School of Business](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Booth_School_of_Business "University of Chicago Booth School of Business") and [Laboratory Schools](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Laboratory_Schools "University of Chicago Laboratory Schools") can be seen on the right (east), as the panoramic is bounded on both sides by the [Midway Plaisance](/wiki/Midway_Plaisance "Midway Plaisance") (south).

The main campus of the University of Chicago consists of 217 acres (87.8 ha) in the Chicago neighborhoods of [Hyde Park](/wiki/Hyde_Park,_Chicago "Hyde Park, Chicago") and [Woodlawn](/wiki/Woodlawn,_Chicago "Woodlawn, Chicago"), approximately eight miles (13 km) south of [downtown Chicago](/wiki/Chicago_Loop "Chicago Loop").[[35]](#cite_note-35) The northern and southern portions of campus are separated by the [Midway Plaisance](/wiki/Midway_Plaisance "Midway Plaisance"), a large, linear park created for the 1893 [World's Columbian Exposition](/wiki/World%27s_Columbian_Exposition "World's Columbian Exposition"). In 2011, *[Travel+Leisure](/wiki/Travel%2BLeisure "Travel+Leisure")* listed the university as one of the most beautiful college campuses in the United States.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Aerial shots from the University of Chicago campus

[![A path in the grass leading towards a large, partially ivy-covered building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Harper_Quadrangle.jpg/250px-Harper_Quadrangle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Harper_Quadrangle.jpg)

View of university building from the Harper Quadrangle

The first buildings of the campus, which make up what are now known as the Main Quadrangles, were part of a master plan conceived by two University of Chicago trustees and plotted by Chicago architect [Henry Ives Cobb](/wiki/Henry_Ives_Cobb "Henry Ives Cobb").[[37]](#cite_note-famousbldgs-37) The Main Quadrangles consist of six [quadrangles](/wiki/Quadrangle_(architecture) "Quadrangle (architecture)"), each surrounded by buildings, bordering one larger quadrangle.[[11]](#cite_note-goodspeed-11): 221 The buildings of the Main Quadrangles were designed by Cobb, [Shepley, Rutan and Coolidge](/wiki/Shepley,_Rutan_and_Coolidge "Shepley, Rutan and Coolidge"), [Holabird & Roche](/wiki/Holabird_%26_Roche "Holabird & Roche"), and other architectural firms in a mixture of the [Victorian Gothic](/wiki/Victorian_Gothic "Victorian Gothic") and [Collegiate Gothic](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic "Collegiate Gothic") styles, patterned on the colleges of the [University of Oxford](/wiki/University_of_Oxford "University of Oxford").[[37]](#cite_note-famousbldgs-37) Mitchell Tower, for example, is modeled after Oxford's [Magdalen Tower](/wiki/Magdalen_Tower "Magdalen Tower"),[[38]](#cite_note-mitchell-38) and the university Commons, [Hutchinson Hall](/wiki/Hutchinson_Hall "Hutchinson Hall"), replicates [Christ Church](/wiki/Christ_Church,_Oxford "Christ Church, Oxford") Hall.[[39]](#cite_note-39) In celebration of the 2018 Illinois Bicentennial, the University of Chicago Quadrangles were selected as one of the Illinois 200 Great Places by the [American Institute of Architects](/wiki/American_Institute_of_Architects "American Institute of Architects") Illinois component.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

[![A comparison between the similar architectures of Chicago's Mitchell Tower on the left and Oxford's Magdalen Tower on the right.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Mitchell-Magdalen_comparison.jpg/250px-Mitchell-Magdalen_comparison.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mitchell-Magdalen_comparison.jpg)

Many older buildings of the University of Chicago employ [Collegiate Gothic](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic "Collegiate Gothic") architecture like that of the University of Oxford. For example, Chicago's Mitchell Tower (left) was modeled after Oxford's [Magdalen Tower](/wiki/Magdalen_Tower,_Oxford "Magdalen Tower, Oxford") (right).

After the 1940s, the campus's Gothic style began to give way to modern styles.[[37]](#cite_note-famousbldgs-37) In 1955, [Eero Saarinen](/wiki/Eero_Saarinen "Eero Saarinen") was contracted to develop a second master plan, which led to the construction of buildings both north and south of the Midway, including the Laird Bell Law Quadrangle (a complex designed by Saarinen);[[37]](#cite_note-famousbldgs-37) a series of arts buildings;[[37]](#cite_note-famousbldgs-37) Edith Abbott Hall, designed by [Ludwig Mies van der Rohe](/wiki/Ludwig_Mies_van_der_Rohe "Ludwig Mies van der Rohe");[[42]](#cite_note-42) the Keller Center, which is home of the [Harris School of Public Policy](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Harris_School_of_Public_Policy "University of Chicago Harris School of Public Policy") and was designed by [Edward Durrell Stone](/wiki/Edward_Durrell_Stone "Edward Durrell Stone");[[43]](#cite_note-43) and the [Regenstein Library](/wiki/Regenstein_Library "Regenstein Library"), the largest building on campus, a [brutalist](/wiki/Brutalist_architecture "Brutalist architecture") structure designed by [Walter Netsch](/wiki/Walter_Netsch "Walter Netsch").[[44]](#cite_note-44) Another master plan, designed in 1999 and updated in 2004,[[45]](#cite_note-twentytwenty-45) produced the [Gerald Ratner Athletics Center](/wiki/Gerald_Ratner_Athletics_Center "Gerald Ratner Athletics Center") (2003),[[45]](#cite_note-twentytwenty-45) the [Max Palevsky Residential Commons](/wiki/Max_Palevsky_Residential_Commons "Max Palevsky Residential Commons") (2001),[[37]](#cite_note-famousbldgs-37) [South Campus Residence Hall](/wiki/South_Campus_Residence_Hall "South Campus Residence Hall") and dining commons (2009), a new children's hospital,[[46]](#cite_note-milestones-46) and other construction, expansions, and restorations.[[47]](#cite_note-47) In 2011, the university completed the glass dome-shaped [Joe and Rika Mansueto Library](/wiki/Joe_and_Rika_Mansueto_Library "Joe and Rika Mansueto Library"), which provides a grand reading room for the university library and prevents the need for an off-campus book depository.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

The site of [Chicago Pile-1](/wiki/Chicago_Pile-1 "Chicago Pile-1") is a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") and is marked by the [Henry Moore](/wiki/Henry_Moore "Henry Moore") sculpture *[Nuclear Energy](/wiki/Nuclear_Energy_(sculpture) "Nuclear Energy (sculpture)")*.[[49]](#cite_note-49) [Robie House](/wiki/Robie_House "Robie House"), a [Frank Lloyd Wright](/wiki/Frank_Lloyd_Wright "Frank Lloyd Wright") building acquired by the university in 1963, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site,[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) as well as a National Historic Landmark,[[52]](#cite_note-52) as is room 405 of the [George Herbert Jones Laboratory](/wiki/George_Herbert_Jones_Laboratory "George Herbert Jones Laboratory"), where [Glenn T. Seaborg](/wiki/Glenn_T._Seaborg "Glenn T. Seaborg") and his team were the first to isolate plutonium.[[53]](#cite_note-53) [Hitchcock Hall](/wiki/Hitchcock_Hall "Hitchcock Hall"), an undergraduate dormitory, is on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places").[[54]](#cite_note-nrhp-54)

Adjacent to the campus in [Jackson Park](/wiki/Jackson_Park_(Chicago) "Jackson Park (Chicago)") is the home of the [Obama Presidential Center](/wiki/Barack_Obama_Presidential_Center "Barack Obama Presidential Center"), the Presidential Library for the 44th president of the United States[[55]](#cite_note-55) with expected completion in 2026. The Obamas settled in the university's Hyde Park neighborhood, where they raised their children and where [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") began his political career. [Michelle Obama](/wiki/Michelle_Obama "Michelle Obama") served as an administrator at the university and founded the university's Community Service Center.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

#### Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![Two University of Chicago shuttle buses (with "UGo" red branding on the sides) on 57th Street outside the Regenstein Library during a snowstorm](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/UChicago_UGo_Busses_in_Snow.png/250px-UChicago_UGo_Busses_in_Snow.png)](/wiki/File:UChicago_UGo_Busses_in_Snow.png)

Two University of Chicago shuttle buses on 57th Street outside [Regenstein Library](/wiki/Regenstein_Library "Regenstein Library")

The Hyde Park campus is served by the CTA [Red Line](/wiki/Red_Line_(CTA) "Red Line (CTA)") and [Green Line](/wiki/Green_Line_(CTA) "Green Line (CTA)"), as well as by the [Metra Electric District](/wiki/Metra_Electric_District "Metra Electric District") and [South Shore Line](/wiki/South_Shore_Line "South Shore Line") commuter trains,[[59]](#cite_note-:73-59) all of which provide access to downtown Chicago.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-62)[[63]](#cite_note-63) The campus is also served by a network of [CTA bus routes](/wiki/CTA_bus_routes "CTA bus routes").[[59]](#cite_note-:73-59)

The university shuttle program includes daytime and nighttime routes, most of which operate within Hyde Park.[[64]](#cite_note-:82-64) In 2022, the university added a Downtown Campus Connector to its shuttle program, which connects the main Hyde Park campus to the Gleacher Center and downtown UChicago Medicine clinics.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

In 2024, the university introduced a [Via](/wiki/Via_Transportation "Via Transportation") ride-sharing program ahead of the 2024–2025 school year, which provides unlimited free rides on campus in shared vans.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

#### Safety

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Safety")]

In November 2021, a university graduate was robbed and fatally shot on a sidewalk in a residential area in Hyde Park near campus; a total of three University of Chicago students were killed by gunfire incidents in 2021.[[67]](#cite_note-Suspect_Charged-67)[[68]](#cite_note-could_be_anyone-68) These incidents prompted student protests and an open letter to university leadership signed by more than 300 faculty members.[[69]](#cite_note-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70) In response, the university introduced measures including increased foot and vehicular patrols near campus, expanded coordination between the [university police department](/wiki/University_Police_Department "University Police Department") and the [Chicago Police Department](/wiki/Chicago_Police_Department "Chicago Police Department"), and greater use of [security cameras](/wiki/Closed-circuit_television "Closed-circuit television") and [license plate readers](/wiki/Automatic_number-plate_recognition "Automatic number-plate recognition").[[71]](#cite_note-71) The university continues to maintain one of the largest private police forces in the country.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

### Satellite campuses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Satellite campuses")]

[![A curved, glass walkway over a pavilion leading to a building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/The_University_of_Chicago_Francis_and_Rose_Yuen_Campus.jpg/250px-The_University_of_Chicago_Francis_and_Rose_Yuen_Campus.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_University_of_Chicago_Francis_and_Rose_Yuen_Campus.jpg)

The University of Chicago [Francis and Rose Yuen Campus](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Hong_Kong "University of Chicago Hong Kong"), located at [Mount Davis, Hong Kong](/wiki/Mount_Davis,_Hong_Kong "Mount Davis, Hong Kong")

The university also maintains facilities apart from its main campus. The university's [Booth School of Business](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Booth_School_of_Business "University of Chicago Booth School of Business") maintains campuses in [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong "Hong Kong"), [London](/wiki/London "London"), and downtown Chicago.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The Center in Paris, a campus located on the [left bank](/wiki/Rive_Gauche "Rive Gauche") of the [Seine](/wiki/Seine "Seine") in Paris, hosts various undergraduate and graduate study programs.[[74]](#cite_note-74) The university also maintains the Chicago House, based in [Luxor](/wiki/Luxor "Luxor"), which serves as the Egyptian headquarters for the [Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures](/wiki/Institute_for_the_Study_of_Ancient_Cultures "Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures").[[75]](#cite_note-75) In fall 2010, the university opened a center in [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"), near [Renmin University](/wiki/Renmin_University "Renmin University")'s campus in [Haidian District](/wiki/Haidian_District "Haidian District"). The most recent additions are a center in [New Delhi](/wiki/New_Delhi "New Delhi"), India, which opened in 2014,[[76]](#cite_note-76) and a center in Hong Kong which opened in 2018.[[77]](#cite_note-77) In 2024, the university opened the John W. Boyer Center in Paris, designed by architectural firm [Studio Gang](/wiki/Studio_Gang "Studio Gang") and nearly tripling the size of the Center in Paris which had opened in 2003.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Academics")]

[![A large gate, with students walking through](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/University_of_Chicago_July_2013_19_%28Main_Quadrangles%29.jpg/250px-University_of_Chicago_July_2013_19_%28Main_Quadrangles%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Chicago_July_2013_19_(Main_Quadrangles).jpg)

The University of Chicago Main Quadrangles, looking north

The academic bodies of the University of Chicago consist of the [college](/wiki/College_of_the_University_of_Chicago "College of the University of Chicago"), four divisions of graduate research, seven professional schools, and the [Graham School of Continuing Liberal and Professional Studies](/wiki/Graham_School_of_Continuing_Liberal_and_Professional_Studies "Graham School of Continuing Liberal and Professional Studies").[[79]](#cite_note-79) The university also contains a library system, the [University of Chicago Press](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Press "University of Chicago Press"), and the [University of Chicago Medical Center](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Medical_Center "University of Chicago Medical Center"), and oversees several laboratories, including the [Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory](/wiki/Fermilab "Fermilab") (Fermilab), the [Argonne National Laboratory](/wiki/Argonne_National_Laboratory "Argonne National Laboratory"), and the [Marine Biological Laboratory](/wiki/Marine_Biological_Laboratory "Marine Biological Laboratory").[[80]](#cite_note-:5-80) The university is accredited by the [Higher Learning Commission](/wiki/The_Higher_Learning_Commission "The Higher Learning Commission").[[81]](#cite_note-collegenavigator-81) It is a member of the [National Association of Independent Colleges and Universities](/wiki/National_Association_of_Independent_Colleges_and_Universities "National Association of Independent Colleges and Universities") and the [Universities Research Association](/wiki/Universities_Research_Association "Universities Research Association").[[82]](#cite_note-82)[[83]](#cite_note-83)

The university runs on a [quarter system](/wiki/Quarter_system "Quarter system") in which the academic year is divided into four terms: Summer (June–August), Autumn (September–December), Winter (January–March), and Spring (March–June).[[84]](#cite_note-:10-84) Full-time undergraduate students take three to four courses every quarter[[85]](#cite_note-85) for approximately ten weeks before their quarterly academic breaks. The school year typically begins in late September and ends in early June.[[84]](#cite_note-:10-84)

### Undergraduate college

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Undergraduate college")]

Main article: [College of the University of Chicago](/wiki/College_of_the_University_of_Chicago "College of the University of Chicago")

[![The campus in winter, with snow covering the ground](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Harper_Library_from_the_Midway_Plaisance.JPG/250px-Harper_Library_from_the_Midway_Plaisance.JPG)](/wiki/File:Harper_Library_from_the_Midway_Plaisance.JPG)

Harper Memorial Library was dedicated in 1912, and its architecture takes inspiration from various colleges in England.

The College of the University of Chicago grants [Bachelor of Arts](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Arts "Bachelor of Arts") and [Bachelor of Science](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Science "Bachelor of Science") degrees in 51 undergraduate courses of study[[86]](#cite_note-86) (since 2005 known as [majors](/wiki/Academic_major "Academic major")) and 33 secondary courses of study, now known as minors.[[87]](#cite_note-87) The college's academics are divided into four divisions: the Biological Sciences Collegiate Division, the Physical Sciences Collegiate Division, the Social Sciences Collegiate Division, and the Humanities Collegiate Division. Each division is affiliated with the corresponding graduate division of the university.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

The college introduced a now-widespread model of the liberal arts undergraduate program which featured the [Socratic method](/wiki/Socratic_method "Socratic method") in undergraduate contexts, the [Great Books](/wiki/Great_Books_of_the_Western_World#History "Great Books of the Western World") program, and the [core curriculum](/wiki/Core_curriculum "Core curriculum").[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)[[91]](#cite_note-91) Since the 1999–2000 school year, 15 courses across seven subjects and demonstrated proficiency in a foreign language are required under the core curriculum.[[92]](#cite_note-:6-92)

[![A large building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Eckhart_Hall.jpg/250px-Eckhart_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eckhart_Hall.jpg)

Eckhart Hall houses the university's math department.

### Graduate research divisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Graduate research divisions")]

Further information: [Divisions of the University of Chicago](/wiki/Divisions_of_the_University_of_Chicago "Divisions of the University of Chicago")

The university's academic departments, committees, and institutes are organized into four graduate research divisions: the Arts & Humanities Division, the Biological Sciences Division, the Physical Sciences Division, and the Social Sciences Division.[[93]](#cite_note-93)

In the autumn quarter of 2022, the university enrolled 10,546 graduate students on degree-seeking courses: 569 in the Biological Sciences Division, 612 in the Humanities Division, 2,103 in the Physical Sciences Division, 972 in the Social Sciences Division, and 6,290 in the professional schools (including the Graham School).[[94]](#cite_note-94) The university is home to several committees for interdisciplinary scholarship, including the [Committee on Social Thought](/wiki/Committee_on_Social_Thought "Committee on Social Thought").[[95]](#cite_note-95)

#### Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Research")]

[![Bird's-eye view of a large circular shape inscribed into a grassy area](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Fermilab.jpg/250px-Fermilab.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fermilab.jpg)

Aerial view of [Fermilab](/wiki/Fermilab "Fermilab"), a science research laboratory co-managed by the University of Chicago

According to the [National Science Foundation](/wiki/National_Science_Foundation "National Science Foundation"), the University of Chicago spent $423.9 million on research and development in 2018, ranking it 60th in the nation.[[96]](#cite_note-96) It is [classified](/wiki/Carnegie_Classification_of_Institutions_of_Higher_Education "Carnegie Classification of Institutions of Higher Education") among "R1: Doctoral Universities – Very high research activity".[[97]](#cite_note-97) It is a founding member of the [Association of American Universities](/wiki/Association_of_American_Universities "Association of American Universities"), and was a member of the Committee on Institutional Cooperation between 1946 and 2016, when the group's name was changed to the [Big Ten Academic Alliance](/wiki/Big_Ten_Academic_Alliance "Big Ten Academic Alliance"). The University of Chicago is not a member of the rebranded consortium, but continues to be a collaborator.[[98]](#cite_note-btaa_chicago-98)[[99]](#cite_note-99)

The university operates more than 140 research centers and institutes on campus. Among these are the [Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures, West Asia & North Africa](/wiki/Institute_for_the_Study_of_Ancient_Cultures "Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures")—a museum and research center for [Near Eastern](/wiki/Near_East "Near East") studies owned and operated by the university—and a number of [National Resource Centers](/wiki/National_Resource_Center "National Resource Center"), including the [Center for Middle Eastern Studies](/wiki/Center_for_Middle_Eastern_Studies_at_the_University_of_Chicago "Center for Middle Eastern Studies at the University of Chicago").[[100]](#cite_note-100) Chicago also operates or is affiliated with several research institutions apart from the university proper. The university manages the [Argonne National Laboratory](/wiki/Argonne_National_Laboratory "Argonne National Laboratory"), part of the [United States Department of Energy](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Energy "United States Department of Energy")'s national laboratory system, and co-manages the [Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory](/wiki/Fermilab "Fermilab") (Fermilab), a nearby particle physics laboratory.[[80]](#cite_note-:5-80) It was also part of the Astrophysical Research Consortium that constructed the [Apache Point Observatory](/wiki/Apache_Point_Observatory "Apache Point Observatory") in [Sunspot, New Mexico](/wiki/Sunspot,_New_Mexico "Sunspot, New Mexico").[[101]](#cite_note-101) Faculty and students at the adjacent [Toyota Technological Institute at Chicago](/wiki/Toyota_Technological_Institute_at_Chicago "Toyota Technological Institute at Chicago") collaborate with the university.[[102]](#cite_note-102) In 2013, the university formed an affiliation with the formerly independent [Marine Biological Laboratory](/wiki/Marine_Biological_Laboratory "Marine Biological Laboratory") in Woods Hole, Mass.[[103]](#cite_note-103) The [National Opinion Research Center](/wiki/National_Opinion_Research_Center "National Opinion Research Center") maintains an office at the Hyde Park campus and is affiliated with multiple academic centers and institutes.[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105)

[![Ivy-colored building with various shades of color](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/University_of_Chicago_at_Fall.jpg/250px-University_of_Chicago_at_Fall.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Chicago_at_Fall.jpg)

University of Chicago building during fall

The University of Chicago has been the site of various experiments and academic movements. The university has played a role in shaping ideas about the [free market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market")[[106]](#cite_note-106) and is the namesake of the [Chicago school of economics](/wiki/Chicago_school_of_economics "Chicago school of economics"), the school of economic thought supported by [Milton Friedman](/wiki/Milton_Friedman "Milton Friedman") and other economists. The university's [sociology](/wiki/Sociology "Sociology") department was the first independent sociology department in the United States and gave birth to the [Chicago school of sociology](/wiki/Chicago_school_(sociology) "Chicago school (sociology)").[[107]](#cite_note-107) The university was the site of the [Chicago Pile-1](/wiki/Chicago_Pile-1 "Chicago Pile-1") (the first controlled, self-sustaining human-made nuclear chain reaction, part of the [Manhattan Project](/wiki/Manhattan_Project "Manhattan Project")), of [Robert Millikan](/wiki/Robert_Millikan "Robert Millikan")'s [oil-drop experiment](/wiki/Oil-drop_experiment "Oil-drop experiment") that calculated the charge of the electron,[[108]](#cite_note-oildrop-108) and of the development of [radiocarbon dating](/wiki/Radiocarbon_dating "Radiocarbon dating") by [Willard F. Libby](/wiki/Willard_F._Libby "Willard F. Libby") in 1946.[[109]](#cite_note-109) The chemical experiment that tested how life originated on early Earth, the [Miller–Urey experiment](/wiki/Miller%E2%80%93Urey_experiment "Miller–Urey experiment"), was also conducted at the university.[[110]](#cite_note-110) [REM sleep](/wiki/REM_sleep "REM sleep") was discovered at the university in 1953 by [Nathaniel Kleitman](/wiki/Nathaniel_Kleitman "Nathaniel Kleitman") and [Eugene Aserinsky](/wiki/Eugene_Aserinsky "Eugene Aserinsky").[[111]](#cite_note-111)

The University of Chicago Department of Astronomy and Astrophysics operated the [Yerkes Observatory](/wiki/Yerkes_Observatory "Yerkes Observatory") in [Williams Bay, Wisconsin](/wiki/Williams_Bay,_Wisconsin "Williams Bay, Wisconsin") from 1897 until 2018,[[112]](#cite_note-112) where the largest operating [refracting telescope](/wiki/Refracting_telescope "Refracting telescope") in the world and other telescopes are located.[[113]](#cite_note-113)

### Professional schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Professional schools")]

The university contains seven professional schools, as well as the [Graham School of Continuing Liberal and Professional Studies](/wiki/Graham_School_of_Continuing_Liberal_and_Professional_Studies "Graham School of Continuing Liberal and Professional Studies").

* The [University of Chicago Divinity School](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Divinity_School "University of Chicago Divinity School") was the first professional school at the University of Chicago, chartered in 1865 and incorporated into the university in 1890. It offers four graduate degree programs as well as undergraduate course offerings.[[114]](#cite_note-114) It has been accredited by the [Association of Theological Schools in the United States and Canada](/wiki/Association_of_Theological_Schools_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Association of Theological Schools in the United States and Canada") since 1938.[[115]](#cite_note-collegenavigator2-115)
* The [Booth School of Business](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Booth_School_of_Business "University of Chicago Booth School of Business") was founded in 1898 as the College of Commerce and Politics and received business school accreditation in 1916.[[116]](#cite_note-116)[[117]](#cite_note-117) In 2008, the then-called Graduate School of Business was renamed following a $300 million donation from alumnus David Booth.[[118]](#cite_note-:8-118) It was ranked fourth out of 133 American business schools by U.S. News in 2025.[[119]](#cite_note-119)
* The [University of Chicago Law School](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Law_School "University of Chicago Law School") was established in 1902, twelve years after the founding of the university.[[120]](#cite_note-120) It has been accredited by the [American Bar Association](/wiki/American_Bar_Association "American Bar Association") since 1923 and was ranked third out of 195 American law schools by U.S. News in 2025.[[115]](#cite_note-collegenavigator2-115)[[121]](#cite_note-121)
* The [Crown Family School of Social Work, Policy, and Practice](/wiki/Crown_Family_School_of_Social_Work,_Policy,_and_Practice "Crown Family School of Social Work, Policy, and Practice") was first established in 1908 as the Chicago School of Civics and Philanthropy and received its first dean, [Edith Abbott](/wiki/Edith_Abbott "Edith Abbott"), who became the first female dean of any graduate school in the United States in 1924. It was renamed in 2021 in recognition of a $75 million donation from [James](/wiki/James_Crown "James Crown") and Paula Crown and the Crown family.[[122]](#cite_note-122)[[123]](#cite_note-123)
* The [Pritzker School of Medicine](/wiki/Pritzker_School_of_Medicine "Pritzker School of Medicine") matriculated its first class of medical students in 1927 and was renamed to the Pritzker School of Medicine in 1968 in recognition of support from the [Pritzker family](/wiki/Pritzker_family "Pritzker family").[[124]](#cite_note-124) It has been accredited by the [Liaison Committee on Medical Education](/wiki/Liaison_Committee_on_Medical_Education "Liaison Committee on Medical Education") since 1942.[[115]](#cite_note-collegenavigator2-115) In 2023, the school declined to continue submitting data to U.S. News to help the publication rank the institution, joining medical schools including those at Harvard, Stanford, and Columbia in doing so.[[125]](#cite_note-125)
* The [Harris School of Public Policy](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Harris_School_of_Public_Policy "University of Chicago Harris School of Public Policy") was established in 1988 as the Graduate School of Public Policy Studies. In 1990, it was renamed in recognition of [Irving Harris](/wiki/Irving_Harris "Irving Harris")' financial support of the program during its inception.[[126]](#cite_note-126) The school offers six graduate degree programs as well as joint degree and non-degree programs.[[127]](#cite_note-127)
* The [Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering](/wiki/Pritzker_School_of_Molecular_Engineering "Pritzker School of Molecular Engineering") was founded in 2019 following an expansion of the Institute of Molecular Engineering, which was established in 2011. The Pritzker Foundation provided a $75 million donation to help establish the school, which occupies the William Eckhardt Research Center.[[128]](#cite_note-128)
* The [Graham School of Continuing Liberal and Professional Studies](/wiki/Graham_School_of_Continuing_Liberal_and_Professional_Studies "Graham School of Continuing Liberal and Professional Studies"), originally known as the university-Extension program, was established in 1892.[[129]](#cite_note-129) The school offers various non-degree courses and certificates as well as degree programs.[[130]](#cite_note-130) In 1997, it was renamed to the William B. and Catherine V. Graham School of General Studies in honor of a $10 million donation from William and Catherine Graham made in the same year.[[131]](#cite_note-131)

Until 1989, the [University of Chicago Graduate Library School](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Graduate_Library_School "University of Chicago Graduate Library School") was the graduate-level librarianship school at the University of Chicago. It was established in 1928 to develop a program for the graduate education of librarians with a focus on research.[[132]](#cite_note-132) Housed for a time in the [Joseph Regenstein Library](/wiki/Regenstein_Library "Regenstein Library"), the Graduate Library School closed in 1989 when the University of Chicago decided to promote information studies instead of professional education.[[133]](#cite_note-133)[[134]](#cite_note-134)

### Associated institutions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Associated institutions")]

[![A large building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/University_of_Chicago_Laboratory_Schools_exterior.jpg/250px-University_of_Chicago_Laboratory_Schools_exterior.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Chicago_Laboratory_Schools_exterior.jpg)

The University of Chicago Laboratory Schools, a private day school run by the university

The university runs a number of academic institutions and programs apart from its undergraduate and postgraduate schools. It operates the [University of Chicago Laboratory Schools](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Laboratory_Schools "University of Chicago Laboratory Schools") (a private [day school](/wiki/Day_school "Day school") for [K-12](/wiki/K%E2%80%9312_(education) "K–12 (education)") students and [day care](/wiki/Day_care "Day care")),[[135]](#cite_note-135) and a public [charter school](/wiki/Charter_school "Charter school") with three campuses on the [South Side](/wiki/South_Side_(Chicago) "South Side (Chicago)") of Chicago administered by the university's Urban Education Institute.[[136]](#cite_note-136) In addition, the Hyde Park Day School, a school for students with learning disabilities,[[137]](#cite_note-137) and the [Sonia Shankman Orthogenic School](/wiki/Sonia_Shankman_Orthogenic_School "Sonia Shankman Orthogenic School"), a residential treatment program for those with behavioral and emotional problems,[[138]](#cite_note-138) maintains a location on the University of Chicago campus. Since 1983, the University of Chicago has maintained the [University of Chicago School Mathematics Project](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_School_Mathematics_Project "University of Chicago School Mathematics Project"), a mathematics program used in urban primary and secondary schools.[[139]](#cite_note-139) The university runs a program called the Council on Advanced Studies, which administers interdisciplinary workshops to provide a forum for graduate students, faculty, and visiting scholars to present scholarly work in progress.[[140]](#cite_note-140) The university also operates the [University of Chicago Press](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Press "University of Chicago Press"), the largest [university press](/wiki/University_press "University press") in North America.[[141]](#cite_note-141)[[142]](#cite_note-142)

### Library system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Library system")]

Main article: [University of Chicago Library](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Library "University of Chicago Library")

[![The interior of the reading room of the library, with tables and lamps on either side of the hall](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/University_of_Chicago%2C_Harper_Library.jpg/250px-University_of_Chicago%2C_Harper_Library.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_of_Chicago,_Harper_Library.jpg)

University of Chicago, Harper Library

The [University of Chicago Library](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Library "University of Chicago Library") system encompasses six libraries that contain a total of 11 million volumes, the 9th most among library systems in the United States.[[143]](#cite_note-143) The university's primary library is the [Regenstein Library](/wiki/Regenstein_Library "Regenstein Library"), which contains over 4.5 million print volumes on a variety of subjects and is the largest on campus.[[144]](#cite_note-144)[[145]](#cite_note-145) The [Joe and Rika Mansueto Library](/wiki/Joe_and_Rika_Mansueto_Library "Joe and Rika Mansueto Library"), built in 2011, houses a large study space and an automated book storage and retrieval system.[[146]](#cite_note-146) The [John Crerar Library](/wiki/John_Crerar_Library "John Crerar Library") contains more than 1.4 million volumes in the biological, medical and physical sciences and collections in general science and the philosophy and history of science, medicine, and technology.[[147]](#cite_note-147) The university also operates a number of special libraries, including the D'Angelo Law Library, the Social Service Administration Library, and the Eckhart Library for mathematics and computer science.[[148]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-148)[[149]](#cite_note-petersons-149) The Harper Memorial Library, the first library of the university, is now a reading and study room.[[150]](#cite_note-150)

### Arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Arts")]

[![A large building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6c/Logan_Center_for_the_Arts.jpg/250px-Logan_Center_for_the_Arts.jpg)](/wiki/File:Logan_Center_for_the_Arts.jpg)

The Reva and David Logan Center for the Arts, opened in 2012

The University of Chicago Arts program joins academic departments in the Division of the Arts & Humanities and the undergraduate College, student art programs, and professional organizations including the [Court Theatre](/wiki/Court_Theatre_(Chicago) "Court Theatre (Chicago)"), the [Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures](/wiki/Institute_for_the_Study_of_Ancient_Cultures "Institute for the Study of Ancient Cultures"), the [Smart Museum of Art](/wiki/Smart_Museum_of_Art "Smart Museum of Art"), and the [Renaissance Society](/wiki/Renaissance_Society "Renaissance Society").[[151]](#cite_note-151) The university offers graduate degrees in music, cinema and media studies, visual arts, and the humanities, among other subjects.[[152]](#cite_note-152)[[153]](#cite_note-153) It also offers bachelor's degree programs in visual arts, music, art history, cinema and media studies, and theater and performance studies.[[154]](#cite_note-154) Several thousand major and non-major undergraduates enroll annually in creative and performing arts classes.[[155]](#cite_note-155)

The university was home to the improvisational [Compass Players](/wiki/Compass_Players "Compass Players") student comedy troupe, which evolved into [The Second City](/wiki/The_Second_City "The Second City") in 1959.[[156]](#cite_note-156)[[157]](#cite_note-157) The university has an artist-in-residence program, which has supported over 32 individual artists as of May 2025.[[158]](#cite_note-158) The Reva and David Logan Center for the Arts opened in 2012. It was financed by a $35 million gift from alumnus David Logan and his wife Reva, the single largest cash gift to the arts in the city of Chicago as of 2025. The center includes spaces for exhibitions, performances, classes, and media production.[[159]](#cite_note-159)
