The University of Chicago is considered one of the most prestigious research universities in the United States.[[168]](#cite_note-reputation-168)The *[Academic Ranking of World Universities](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities")* has consistently placed the University of Chicago among the top 10 universities in the world.[[169]](#cite_note-169) In 2026, the university was ranked 6th by [*US News & World Report*](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report_Best_Colleges_Ranking "U.S. News & World Report Best Colleges Ranking") and 13th by *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")*.[[170]](#cite_note-170)[[171]](#cite_note-171) In 2025, *[QS World University Rankings](/wiki/QS_World_University_Rankings "QS World University Rankings")* placed the university in 13th place worldwide, while [*THE World University Rankings*](/wiki/Times_Higher_Education_World_University_Rankings "Times Higher Education World University Rankings") ranked the university in a tie for 14th.[[172]](#cite_note-172)[[173]](#cite_note-173)
In 2026, Chicago was ranked 5th in the world universities ranking by [*Time*](/wiki/Time_(magazine) "Time (magazine)") magazine and [Statista](/wiki/Statista "Statista").[[174]](#cite_note-174)

The university's [law](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Law_School "University of Chicago Law School") and [business](/wiki/Booth_School_of_Business "Booth School of Business") schools consistently rank among the top three professional schools in the United States. In 2025, the business school was placed in second out of 77 American schools by *[Bloomberg](/wiki/Bloomberg_News "Bloomberg News")*,[[175]](#cite_note-175) fourth in the US by *US News & World Report,*[[176]](#cite_note-176) and second by *[Fortune](/wiki/Fortune_(magazine) "Fortune (magazine)").*[[177]](#cite_note-177) In the same year, it was placed fifteenth in the world by *QS World University Rankings* and seventeenth by the [Financial Times](/wiki/Financial_Times "Financial Times").[[178]](#cite_note-178)[[179]](#cite_note-179) In 2025, the law school was ranked third in the United States by *US News & World Report* and second by [*Above the Law*](/wiki/Above_the_Law_(website) "Above the Law (website)").[[180]](#cite_note-180)[[181]](#cite_note-181) In the same year, it was ranked 11th globally by *QS World University Rankings.*[[182]](#cite_note-182)

## Administration and finance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Administration and finance")]

See also: [List of presidents of the University of Chicago](/wiki/List_of_presidents_of_the_University_of_Chicago "List of presidents of the University of Chicago")

[![A professional photo of Paul Alivisatos smiling at the camera](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Paul_Alivisatos_University_of_Chicago_President_September_2021.png/250px-Paul_Alivisatos_University_of_Chicago_President_September_2021.png)](/wiki/File:Paul_Alivisatos_University_of_Chicago_President_September_2021.png)

Paul Alivisatos, University of Chicago president

The university is governed by a board of trustees. The board oversees the long-term development and plans of the university and manages fundraising efforts, and is composed of 55 members including the [university president](/wiki/President_of_the_University_of_Chicago "President of the University of Chicago").[[183]](#cite_note-183) Directly beneath the president are the provost, fourteen vice presidents, including the chief financial officer and [chief investment officer](/wiki/Chief_Investment_Officer "Chief Investment Officer"), and twelve deans.[[184]](#cite_note-184) The current chair of the board of trustees is [David Rubenstein](/wiki/David_Rubenstein "David Rubenstein"), who has occupied the position since May 2022.[[185]](#cite_note-185) The current provost is [Katherine Baicker](/wiki/Katherine_Baicker "Katherine Baicker"), who was appointed in March 2023.[[186]](#cite_note-186)[[187]](#cite_note-187) The current president of the University of Chicago is chemist [Paul Alivisatos](/wiki/Paul_Alivisatos "Paul Alivisatos"), who assumed the role on September 1, 2021.[[188]](#cite_note-188)

The university's endowment was the 21st largest among American educational institutions and state university systems in 2024, valued at roughly $10.1 billion.[[189]](#cite_note-NACUBO-189) Since 2016, the university's board of trustees has resisted pressure from students and faculty to divest its investments from fossil fuel companies.[[190]](#cite_note-190) As of 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit), such investments remain a part of the university's endowment.[[191]](#cite_note-191)

In fall 2023, the university employed 3,418 academic staff and 23,217 administrative staff, including those from the [medical center](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Medical_Center "University of Chicago Medical Center").[[192]](#cite_note-192) In 2024, the university's combined annual budget, including the university proper, the medical center, and the marine biological laboratory, stood at $5.2 billion, with the university's operations making up an additional $2.6 billion.[[193]](#cite_note-193) In the same year, the university's total assets were valued at $20.3 billion.[[194]](#cite_note-194)

Part of the financial plan for the university by former university president [Robert Zimmer](/wiki/Robert_Zimmer "Robert Zimmer") was an increase in accumulation of debt to finance large building projects.[[195]](#cite_note-195) This drew both support and criticism from many in the university community.[[196]](#cite_note-196) In 2024, the university budget deficit stood at $288 million despite liquidating assets to cover the gap; the administration announced plans in November of that year to close the deficit over the next four years.[[197]](#cite_note-197)[[198]](#cite_note-:9-198) The financial strain has caused the university to increase its student-faculty ratio, reduce the proportion of classes taught by research faculty, and spend an unusually high percentage of undergraduate tuition on servicing debt.[[198]](#cite_note-:9-198)[[199]](#cite_note-199)

In the summer of 2025, the university announced more than $100 million in budget cuts across capital projects, hiring, and graduate admissions in a response to the growing university deficit, [less federal funding, and uncertainty about international student admissions](/wiki/Education_policy_of_the_second_Trump_administration#Actions_against_universities "Education policy of the second Trump administration").[[200]](#cite_note-200)

## Student body and admissions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Student body and admissions")]

| Undergraduate admissions statistics | |
| --- | --- |
| 2023 entering class[[201]](#cite_note-201)Change vs. 2018   --- | |
| Admit rate | 4.8% (Neutral decrease −3.3) |
| [Yield rate](/wiki/Yield_(college_admissions) "Yield (college admissions)") | 87.9% (Increase +24.2) |

In fall 2025, the university enrolled 7,496 undergraduate students, 11,195 graduate students, and 408 non-degree students.[[202]](#cite_note-202) The college class of 2025 is composed of 53% male and 47% female students. Twenty-seven percent of the class identify as Asian, 19% as Hispanic, and 10% as Black. Eighteen percent of the class is international.[[203]](#cite_note-collegeadmissions.uchicago.edu-203) The university is [need-blind](/wiki/Need-blind "Need-blind") for domestic applicants.[[204]](#cite_note-204)

Admission to the University of Chicago has become highly selective over the past two decades, reflecting changes in the application process, school popularity, and marketing strategy.[[205]](#cite_note-205)[[206]](#cite_note-206)[[207]](#cite_note-207) Between 1996 and 2023, the acceptance rate of the college fell from 71% to 4.7%.[[208]](#cite_note-208)

The middle 50% band of [SAT](/wiki/SAT "SAT") scores for the undergraduate class of 2025 was 1510–1570 (98th–99th percentiles),[[203]](#cite_note-collegeadmissions.uchicago.edu-203) the average [MCAT](/wiki/MCAT "MCAT") score for students entering the Pritzker School of Medicine class of 2024 was 519 (97th percentile),[[209]](#cite_note-209) the median [GMAT](/wiki/GMAT "GMAT") score for students entering the full-time Booth MBA program class of 2023 was 740 (97th percentile),[[210]](#cite_note-210) and the median [LSAT](/wiki/LSAT "LSAT") score for students entering the Law School class of 2021 was 172 (99th percentile).[[211]](#cite_note-211)

In 2018, the University of Chicago attracted national headlines by becoming the first major research university to no longer require SAT or ACT scores from college applicants.[[212]](#cite_note-212)

## Athletics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Athletics")]

[![Logo of the Maroons](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Chicago_Maroons_logo.svg/250px-Chicago_Maroons_logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Chicago_Maroons_logo.svg)

Official athletics logo

Main article: [Chicago Maroons](/wiki/Chicago_Maroons "Chicago Maroons")

The University of Chicago hosts 19 varsity sports teams: 10 men's teams and 9 women's teams, all called the [Maroons](/wiki/Chicago_Maroons "Chicago Maroons"), with 502 students participating in the 2012–2013 school year.[[213]](#cite_note-sportsfacts-213) The Maroons compete in the [NCAA Division III](/wiki/NCAA_Division_III "NCAA Division III") as members of the [University Athletic Association](/wiki/University_Athletic_Association "University Athletic Association") (UAA).[[214]](#cite_note-214) Their mascot is Phil the Phoenix.[[12]](#cite_note-:72-12)

The university was a founding member of the [Big Ten Conference](/wiki/Big_Ten_Conference "Big Ten Conference") and participated in the NCAA Division I men's basketball and football.[[215]](#cite_note-215) In 1935, the University of Chicago reached the Sweet Sixteen.[[213]](#cite_note-sportsfacts-213) In 1935, [Chicago Maroons football](/wiki/Chicago_Maroons_football "Chicago Maroons football") player [Jay Berwanger](/wiki/Jay_Berwanger "Jay Berwanger") became the first winner of the [Heisman Trophy](/wiki/Heisman_Trophy "Heisman Trophy").[[216]](#cite_note-216) However, the university chose to withdraw from the Big Ten Conference in 1946 after University president [Robert Maynard Hutchins](/wiki/Robert_Maynard_Hutchins "Robert Maynard Hutchins") de-emphasized varsity athletics in 1939 and dropped football.[[217]](#cite_note-217) In 1969, Chicago reinstated football as a Division III team, resuming play at the new [Stagg Field](/wiki/Stagg_Field "Stagg Field").[[218]](#cite_note-218)

The University of Chicago is home to the University of Chicago Rugby Football Club (UCRFC).[[219]](#cite_note-219) Since 2022, men's rugby competes in the Division II Great [Midwest Conference (MWC)](/wiki/Midwest_Conference "Midwest Conference") under [National Collegiate Rugby](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Rugby "National Collegiate Rugby"), having previously competed under [USA Rugby](/wiki/USA_Rugby "USA Rugby"). It was ranked 15th in the country at the end of the 2024 fall 15s season, falling to Montana State 19–48 in the Sweet Sixteen NCR DII playoff round. It competes in a [Rugby 7s](/wiki/Rugby_sevens "Rugby sevens") circuit in the spring. It shares its conference with Loyola University Chicago, the University of Illinois Chicago, Northwestern University (for which it competes in a yearly cup, the Hutchins-Scott Cup), DePaul University, and Benedictine University.[[220]](#cite_note-220) A women's club also exists at the university.[[221]](#cite_note-221)

Founded in 1939, UChicago's Sailing Club is one of the oldest in the country.[[222]](#cite_note-222) The team competes in the Midwestern Collegiate Sailing Association conference within the Intercollegiate Sailing Association. From 2022 to 2025, the team won 4 conference championships granting berths to national championship regattas.[[223]](#cite_note-223)

The university is also home to the [ultimate frisbee](/wiki/Ultimate_frisbee "Ultimate frisbee") team UChicago Fission.[[224]](#cite_note-224)

### Student organizations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Student organizations")]

Students at the University of Chicago operate more than 400 clubs and organizations known as Recognized Student Organizations (RSOs).[[226]](#cite_note-228) These include cultural and religious groups, academic clubs and teams, and common-interest organizations.[[227]](#cite_note-229) Among notable student organizations are the nation's longest continuously running student film society [Doc Films](/wiki/Doc_Films "Doc Films"),[[228]](#cite_note-230)[[229]](#cite_note-FilmNews-231) the organizing committee for the [University of Chicago Scavenger Hunt](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Scavenger_Hunt "University of Chicago Scavenger Hunt"), and the weekly student newspaper *[The Chicago Maroon](/wiki/The_Chicago_Maroon "The Chicago Maroon")*.[[230]](#cite_note-232)

[![A large building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/Reynoldsclub.JPG/250px-Reynoldsclub.JPG)](/wiki/File:Reynoldsclub.JPG)

The university's Reynolds Club, the student center

#### Student government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Student government")]

All recognized student organizations are funded by the [University of Chicago Student Government](/wiki/The_University_of_Chicago_Student_Government "The University of Chicago Student Government"). Student Government consists of graduate and undergraduate students elected to represent members from their respective academic units.[[231]](#cite_note-233) It is led by an executive committee, chaired by a president with the assistance of two vice presidents (one for administration and the other for student life) who are elected together as a slate by the student body each spring. As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit), the Undergraduate Student Government annual budget was greater than $2.5 million.[[232]](#cite_note-234)

#### Fraternities and sororities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Fraternities and sororities")]

As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit), there were more than 20 [Greek](/wiki/Greek_fraternity "Greek fraternity") organizations operating on campus.[[233]](#cite_note-235) According to a 2016 *Maroon* article, 19.6% of undergraduates were members of fraternities or sororities.[[234]](#cite_note-236)

### Student housing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Student housing")]

[![An orange brick building with pink window frames and a blue roof](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Blue%2C_orange_and_pink.jpg/250px-Blue%2C_orange_and_pink.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blue,_orange_and_pink.jpg)

[Max Palevsky Residential Commons](/wiki/Max_Palevsky_Residential_Commons "Max Palevsky Residential Commons") is a dormitory completed in 2001 designed by postmodernist Mexican architect [Ricardo Legorreta](/wiki/Ricardo_Legorreta "Ricardo Legorreta").

Main article: [Housing at the University of Chicago](/wiki/Housing_at_the_University_of_Chicago "Housing at the University of Chicago")

On-campus undergraduate students at the University of Chicago participate in a [house system](/wiki/House_system "House system") in which each student is assigned to one of the university's seven [residence hall](/wiki/Residence_hall "Residence hall") buildings and to a smaller community within their residence hall called a "house". There are 48 houses, with an average of 80 students in each house.[[235]](#cite_note-237) The houses are named after former professors and other historical figures in the university community, such as [Eugene Fama](/wiki/Eugene_Fama "Eugene Fama").[[236]](#cite_note-238)

Students are required to live in on-campus housing for the first six quarters of enrollment.[[237]](#cite_note-239) As of the 2024–2025 school year, 58% of undergraduate students live on campus.[[238]](#cite_note-240)

The university owns and manages more than 300 residential units near campus for graduate students.[[239]](#cite_note-241)

### Traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Traditions")]

[![A group of students in front of six shopping carts that have been decorated; there is a student wearing a helmet in each one](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/Scavrace1.jpg/250px-Scavrace1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Scavrace1.jpg)

Qwazy Quad Rally, [Scav Hunt](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Scavenger_Hunt "University of Chicago Scavenger Hunt") 2005

Main articles: [Doc Films](/wiki/Doc_Films "Doc Films") and [University of Chicago Scavenger Hunt](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Scavenger_Hunt "University of Chicago Scavenger Hunt")

Every May since 1987, the University of Chicago has held the [University of Chicago Scavenger Hunt](/wiki/University_of_Chicago_Scavenger_Hunt "University of Chicago Scavenger Hunt"), in which teams of students compete to obtain notoriously esoteric items from a list.[[240]](#cite_note-242) Every January, the university holds a week-long winter festival, Kuviasungnerk/Kangeiko (Kuvia), which includes early morning exercise routines and fitness workshops.[[241]](#cite_note-243) The university also annually holds a carnival and concert called Summer Breeze,[[242]](#cite_note-244) which hosts outside musicians. Ida Noyes Hall is home to [Doc Films](/wiki/Doc_Films "Doc Films"), a student film society founded in 1932 that screens films nightly at the university.[[243]](#cite_note-245) Since 1946, the university has organized the [Latke-Hamantash Debate](/wiki/Latke-Hamantash_Debate "Latke-Hamantash Debate"), which involves humorous discussions about the relative merits and meanings of [latkes](/wiki/Latke "Latke") and [hamantashen](/wiki/Hamantash "Hamantash").[[244]](#cite_note-246) Since 2002, the Ida Noyes Pub has hosted [Trivia Nights](/wiki/Pub_quiz "Pub quiz") for university affiliates each Tuesday.[[245]](#cite_note-247)

## People

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=University_of_Chicago&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: People")]

For a more comprehensive list, see [List of University of Chicago people](/wiki/List_of_University_of_Chicago_people "List of University of Chicago people").

Since the university's establishment in 1890, there have been 101 [Nobel laureates](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_by_university_affiliation "List of Nobel laureates by university affiliation") across all six categories affiliated with the University of Chicago,[[246]](#cite_note-248) 21 of whom were pursuing research or on faculty at the university at the time of their award announcement.[[247]](#cite_note-249) Of these 101 Nobel Prizes, 30 were in [Physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics"), 19 in [Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry"), 13 in [Physiology/Medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine"), 3 in [Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature"), 1 in [Peace](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize"), and 31 in [Economics](/wiki/Nobel_Memorial_Prize_in_Economic_Sciences "Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences"). Chicago faculty and alumni also include ten [Fields Medalists](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal"),[[248]](#cite_note-:0-250) seventeen [National Medal of Science](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Science "National Medal of Science") recipients,[[249]](#cite_note-251) four [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award") winners,[[250]](#cite_note-252) fifty-eight [MacArthur Fellows](/wiki/MacArthur_Fellows_Program "MacArthur Fellows Program"),[[251]](#cite_note-:1-253) five [John Bates Clark Medalists](/wiki/John_Bates_Clark_Medal "John Bates Clark Medal"),[[252]](#cite_note-254) thirty [Marshall Scholars](/wiki/Marshall_Scholars "Marshall Scholars"),[[253]](#cite_note-:2-255) fifty-five [Rhodes Scholars](/wiki/Rhodes_Scholarship "Rhodes Scholarship"),[[254]](#cite_note-Rhodes-256) twenty-seven [Pulitzer Prize](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") winners,[[255]](#cite_note-:3-257) twenty [National Humanities Medalists](/wiki/National_Humanities_Medal "National Humanities Medal"),[[256]](#cite_note-:4-258) and six [Olympic medalists](/wiki/List_of_American_universities_with_Olympic_medals "List of American universities with Olympic medals").[[257]](#cite_note-259)
